title: Maximum Twin Sum of a Linked List
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ proposals for this challenge: balanced in-place linked-list reversal and runtime-target fixed local array. The minimum-memory champion is implemented in C; see crossReferences.

variants.languageReason: The balanced workload is pointer-heavy traversal over up to 1e5 linked-list nodes. C++ gives native pointer rewiring, inlinable helper code, no GC/interpreter overhead, and direct compatibility with LeetCode's ListNode signature.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected for native speed, direct pointer mutation, constant auxiliary memory, and ergonomic LeetCode integration.
Considered for the smallest runtime baseline; it is used for the memory champion, while C++ remains clearer for the default class-based submission.
Considered for native performance and memory safety; the platform-owned linked-list representation makes in-place reversal more ceremony-heavy without improving runtime or memory.
Considered for compiled loops and simple pointer operations; runtime, GC, and write-barrier costs are unnecessary for this pointer workload.
Considered for strong JIT performance; managed object layout, null checks, and runtime overhead are less attractive than C++ for raw linked-list traversal.
Considered because the algorithm is simple; interpreter/VM overhead loses to native traversal at n up to 1e5.

variants.approachJustification: Use fast/slow pointers to find the start of the second half, reverse that half in place, then walk the first half and reversed second half together to compute every twin sum.

variants.languageReason: The runtime target deliberately spends O(n) stack memory on a fixed int buffer. This reduces the work to one linked-list traversal plus a tight contiguous two-index scan, avoids vector heap allocation, and is the best candidate for LeetCode's 0 ms bucket.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected for optimized native loops, fixed local array storage, and the standard LeetCode class signature.
Considered and viable for similar machine code; chosen instead for the memory champion because C++ already gives the required fixed-buffer runtime shape.
Considered for native performance; fixed-buffer code around platform-owned linked nodes is less ergonomic without improving constants.
Considered for compiled loops; bounds checks and runtime baseline are less predictable than C++ fixed-array indexing.
Considered for JIT array loops; runtime/object baseline and allocation behavior are less attractive for chasing 0 ms.
Considered only as simple implementations; interpreter/VM overhead dominates this small hot loop.

variants.approachJustification: Copy node values into a fixed local array, then scan from both ends to compute twin sums with contiguous memory access and no heap allocation.

reasoningSummary: Twin pairs are first-half nodes matched with the second half in reverse order. The balanced solution reverses the second half in place for O(n)/O(1). The runtime solution copies values to a fixed local array to trade O(n) memory for contiguous scanning and a better shot at LeetCode's 0 ms bucket.
